title: Iran develops rail route to ship oil to China bypassing Strait of Hormuz blockade
summary: Reports indicate Iran has begun developing a rail-based oil transportation route to China to circumvent the US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, representing a significant shift in sanctions evasion strategy.
contents:
## Iran Pursues Rail-Based Oil Export to China

### Strategic Shift
Reports emerged that Iran has begun exploring rail-based oil transportation to China as an alternative to maritime exports blocked by the US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. The initiative represents a fundamental shift in Iran's sanctions evasion strategy, moving from maritime to overland routes.

### Route Details
The proposed "Steel Route" would leverage existing rail infrastructure connecting Iran through Central Asian nations to China's western provinces. The route would bypass the Strait of Hormuz entirely, eliminating the vulnerability to US naval interdiction.

### Context and Motivation
Since the US naval blockade began on April 13, Iran has faced near-complete cutoff from its primary revenue source: oil exports. The US Navy has redirected at least 44 commercial vessels as part of blockade enforcement. Prior to the conflict, Iran exported approximately 1.5-1.8 million barrels per day, primarily to China via maritime routes.

### Capacity Limitations
Rail-based oil transport offers significantly lower capacity compared to maritime tanker routes. Analysts estimate that rail could handle only a fraction of Iran's pre-conflict export volume, though even limited exports would provide critical revenue for the regime under siege.

### Impact on Blockade Strategy
The development, if successful, would undermine the effectiveness of the US naval blockade, which Trump has described as "genius" for its economic impact on Tehran. The rail alternative could provide Iran with enough revenue to sustain the standoff longer than US planners anticipated.
